data.title: S&P 500 Falls And Recovers As Geopolitical Pendulum Swings
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) experienced some geopolitical turmoil during the holiday-shortened trading week ending on Friday, 23 January 2026. The index plunged two percent on Tuesday, 20 January 2026, thanks to a one-two punch.

One of the punches was delivered by President Trump's weekend announcement he would impose new tariffs on European nations opposing his initiative to acquire Greenland from Denmark. That action had sent European stock prices lower before contributing to the U.S. stock market's drop. The good news here, however, is that by Thursday, the dispute was resolved and stock prices recovered.

The second punch was delivered by a developing crisis for Japan's economy. On Tuesday, 20 January 2026, the nation's new prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, announced plans for a snap election to secure a mandate for much higher government spending. That's a problem because Japan's central bank has been struggling to control inflation, which the new spending is expected to aggravate. The response by markets has been to send Japanese government bonds and its currency plunging, which has a global impact. This developing crisis has not yet been resolved.

In reaction to all this and other market-moving news, the U.S. stock market rebounded, but not all the way. The S&P 500 closed out the week at 6,915.61, down 0.35% from its previous week's close.

It's not often we can point to geopolitical events as contributing anything more than noise to the trajectory of stock prices. The latest update of the alternative futures chart shows the S&P 500's trajectory is where it would be expected to be for investors focusing on the upcoming future quarter of 2026-Q2.

As for why, one major reason is provided by the CME Group's FedWatch Tool, which continues to project the Fed will hold the Federal Funds Rate steady until 17 June (2026-Q2), when it anticipates a 77% probability for a quarter-point rate cut. The tool forecasts a better than 50% chance of another quarter-point reduction on 28 October (2026-Q4).

As for what else influenced investor expectations for the future during the week that was, here are the week's market-moving headlines:

Tuesday, 20 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil rises on Kazakh supply disruptions, upbeat economic data; Greenland in focus
    • Trump's Greenland threat puts Europe Inc back in tariff crosshairs
      • Trump says he will '100%' follow through on tariff threat - as it happened
      • Trump promises ‘good’ Greenland plan; Europe pushes back - as it happened
  • Fed officials job security to be determined by U.S. Supreme Court:
    • Supreme Court tests limits of Trump's power over the economy
      • Cook case could lead to 'cause' protections for Fed, or a roadmap for dismissals
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • China must strike right balance between demand and supply, says President Xi
      • China hits 2025 GDP growth target on export boom, but can't shake domestic chill
        • China can't make consumers buy goods, so it leans on services to drive economy
        • As US orders fade, Chinese salespeople face tough grind in new markets
        • China’s Trillion-Dollar Illusion
  • BOJ officials signal more hikes to Japan's interest rates ahead:
    • BOJ to signal more rate hikes as yen, politics fuel inflation risks
      • Japan PM Takaichi to call Feb 8 snap election on spending, tax cuts and defence
      • Buyers flee Japanese debt as Takaichi hits the ground spending
    • Japan’s long-bond yields surge to record highs. Why it may be a problem beyond Tokyo
      • Instant View: Japan bond yields soar as election promises stir fiscal fears
  • ECB officials to get new Number Two official, say new tariffs no big deal for Eurozone:
    • Croatia's Vujcic nominated as ECB's next vice president
    • Fresh tariffs to have muted inflation impact in Europe, ECB's Villeroy says
      • European stocks drop the most in two months on Trump tariff threat over Greenland
  • Nasdaq dropped over 2% as Wall Street closed deep in the red on geopolitical jitters
    • Stock market today: Dow plummets 800 points, S&P 500, Nasdaq sink over 2% as Trump's Greenland threats clobber stocks
      • Gold notches record above $4,700/oz, silver hits all-time high

Wednesday, 21 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Trump drops European tariff threat and announces framework Greenland deal - as it happened
    • Trump signs order to limit Wall Street investors in single-family housing
    • WTI oil prices fall as risks from Kazakh production halt subside
    • US construction spending rebounds in October amid renovations
    • US pending home sales plunge to five-month low in December
  • Fed officials expected to hold rates steady until it gets new management:
    • Fed to hold rates through March, and possibly through Powell's tenure - Reuters poll
    • Trump says he may be down to one candidate to head the Fed
  • Bigger trouble developing in Japan, calls for BOJ and JapanGov officials to stabilize currency:
    • Exclusive: Japan should respond 'decisively' to bond selloff, opposition head says
    • Japan's top labour union group urges government to stabilise forex
  • ECB officials getting excited about digital currency:
    • ECB's Panetta says banks will need to issue fully digital private money
  • Wall Street rallied higher after Trump spoke at Davos and eased tariff concerns
    • Wall Street ends higher as investors cheer Greenland framework deal, averted tariffs

Thursday, 22 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • New trade map takes shape in Davos as world adjusts to Trump tariffs
    • Silver rally strains global supply as retail demand surges across Asia
    • US consumer spending rises, labor market remains in lackluster shape
    • Trump's mortgage-backed bond purchases not moving needle on housing costs
    • US third-quarter economic growth revised slightly higher
  • Fed officials' future job security lies in Supreme Court decision, still not seeing getting inflation they predicted from tariffs:
    • US Supreme Court sees risk in Trump running roughshod over Fed
    • Fed's Favorite Inflation Indicator Refuses To Show Any Signs Of Runaway 'Trump Tariff' Costs
  • Bigger trouble developing in China:
    • China's rural banks struggle to sell seized properties despite hefty discounts
  • Bigger exports developing in Japan, worry about U.S. trade policy; BOJ officials going through their options to deal with bond rout:
    • Japan's exports up for fourth straight month, US dip clouds outlook
    • Japan retains cautious economic view, flags risks from US trade policies
    • What are Japan's policy options to soothe its bond rout?
      • Small but rising opposition party warns BOJ raising rates too rapidly
  • ECB officials think they've got everything covered with their monetary policy for the Eurozone:
    • ECB in no hurry to change rates, comfortable with market bets, accounts show
  • Wall Street finishes up as investors buoyed by tariff relief, upbeat data
    • Wall Street finished higher as Greenland fears ease
    • Gold tops $4,900/oz; silver and platinum extend record setting rally
      • Speculative frenzy catapults silver above $100 an ounce
      • Silver rally strains global supply as retail demand surges across Asia

Friday, 23 January 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices rise on Trump's Iran 'armada' comments and Kazakh outage
  • Bigger trouble developing with China:
    • US control of Venezuela oil risks debt restructuring showdown with China
  • BOJ officials say they're really worried about inflation but hold Japan's interest rates steady, try to keep currency from collapsing, Japan's economy sees growth signs:
    • Japan's core inflation slows in December but stays above BOJ goal
    • BOJ keeps rates steady, raises growth and inflation forecasts
      • BOJ signals more hikes in hawkish tilt, warns of action against yield spike
    • Yen choppy on intervention jitters after BOJ strikes hawkish tone
    • Japan's factory activity returns to growth after seven months, PMI shows
  • Eurozone officials back down, ECB officials perfect monetary policies to be potentially challenged by new inflation:
    • EU to suspend 93 billion euro retaliatory trade package against US for 6 months
    • Euro zone business activity expands steadily but price pressures reemerge
  • Dow ends lower after topsy-turvy week, as Intel's outlook weighs on market sentiment
    • Wall Street ended mixed as turbulent week concluded with Intel earnings
      • Intel tumbles as analysts weigh in on supply constraints, gross margin concerns

The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow tool estimate of real GDP growth in the U.S. during 2025-Q4 gained a tick, rising to +5.4% from the +5.3% growth it projected a week earlier.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet's Big Rally: Ranking The Magnificent 7
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) stock, also known as Google, is up 77% over the last year, and the gains are driven by several of the same market themes (e.g., the cloud, AI and ecosystem) fueling the “Magnificent 7’s” dominance versus the rest of the S&P 500 (SP500). Alphabet has been a stalwart position in my portfolios (especially since the overblown narrative that ChatGPT would overthrow Google’s search dominance), and I’m sharing this report to help readers formulate their own forward-looking opinions. Specifically, this report ranks the Mag 7, including a special focus on Alphabet, and then concludes with my strong opinion on portfolio positioning going forward.

Google’s Big Rally: The Mag 7 Megatrend Themes Making It Possible

For starters, Google shares have soared more than 77% over the last year (see table below), and the gains have been driven by many of the same overarching themes benefiting the Mag 7, such as:

  • The Cloud: Over the last two decades, the incredible migration of data (from personal hard drives and company servers into the cloud—basically datacenters) has been spectacular. Companies that missed this trend (e.g., IBM (IBM)) were left behind and companies that got ahead of it (first Amazon (AMZN), then Microsoft (MSFT) and Google) have capitalized incredibly.

  • Artificial Intelligence: This recent theme (in the last few years) has benefited cloud companies even further because the massive amount of data and compute power required by AI means massive ongoing business and revenue growth for cloud companies. It is also only just beginning to open new opportunities for Apple (AAPL) (partnering with Google Gemini), Microsoft (partnering with OpenAI (OPENAI)), and other Mag 7 companies spending massively on Nvidia’s leading chips powering almost everything AI at this point.

  • Digital Advertising: Alphabet’s Google and YouTube services (which are hosted in the cloud and being accelerated by AI) generate truly spectacular advertising revenue, much like other Mag 7 companies who have been able to accelerate advertising in a major way (e.g. Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft and Amazon).

  • Powerful Ecosystem Moats: Google, much like the rest of the Mag 7, has been able to build massive competitive advantages through walled garden ecosystems whereby they keep users captive within their own ecosystems (so as to ward off competition by preventing it from ever gaining any traction).

And based on these trends and competitive advantages, I fully expect the Mag 7 to keep outperforming the rest of the S&P 500 over the next 12-18 months (and beyond) for a few basic reasons:

  • Stronger earnings growth is projected for the Mag 7 (18-23% in 2026) versus ~13% for the other ~493 stocks. In the long-term, share prices tend to follow earnings growth fairly closely, as you can see in the chart below.

  • Ongoing AI momentum, backlog monetization, and capex payoffs will likely continue to drive outsized Mag 7 returns, as recent heavy capex spending (by essentially all of them) is an indication of healthy expected growth ahead.

  • Slower non-AI growth will likely continue to hamper the "S&P 493" as their “older economy” business models will likely continue to grow, on average, just at a slower pace due to their lack of market disruption opportunities (compared to the Mag 7).

About Google:

For a little perspective, Google generates the majority of its revenue (~80%) from search and YouTube advertisements (with ~35% margin), and then from its growing cloud business (~10% of revenue, and with 24% margins), as well as ~10% of revenue from other bets (e.g., its Waymo self-driving vehicles and Verily healthcare services).

Google’s Competitive Advantages:

Alphabet’s competitive advantages stem from its dominant position in search (with roughly 90% global market share), vast data moat (from billions of daily user interactions), and massive economies of scale in infrastructure. Further, Alphabet’s integrated ecosystem (spanning Android, YouTube, Google Cloud, and AI advancements like Gemini) creates powerful network effects and high switching costs (for users and advertisers).

Google’s Growth:

Alphabet is positioned for continuing growth going forward, driven mainly by accelerating monetization of AI (through Gemini integrations across search, YouTube, and Google Workspace) as well as significant expansion in Google Cloud, especially considering ongoing massive backlog conversion and growing AI demand. Plus, Waymo's accelerating commercialization adds some diversification away from core advertising.

Further, Alphabet’s major partnership with Apple (adopting Gemini for Siri) and heavy recent capital expenditures signal healthy ongoing growth.

Google’s Risks (including Valuation):

From a risk standpoint, Alphabet faces continuing antitrust and regulatory scrutiny (which could force divestitures in some of its ad-tech monopolies), as well as recent heavy capex (they could have overspent, depending on how growth plays out). Not to mention, the shares have recently rebound hard (see earlier chart)—which could be a turnoff to some contrarian investors—although trading at 29x forward earnings is not unreasonable, considering the ongoing high revenue growth and significant competitive advantage (as described earlier).

Google Takeaways:

Despite the very strong rally over the last year, Alphabet is still priced reasonably considering its leadership in cloud, dominance in advertising, AI growth trajectory and powerful competitive advantages. This is a company that is positioned to grow faster than the rest of the economy, and the shares will likely remain a leader among Mag 7 stocks for years to come.

Ranking the “Mag 7” versus the S&P 493

With that Alphabet overview in mind, let’s consider how it compares to the other Magnificent 7 stocks and the rest of the S&P 500 (the “S&P 493”). For starters, I fully expect the Mag 7 to outperform the S&P493 (on average) in the years ahead, simply because Mag 7 stocks are growing their earnings faster.

Understandably, a lot of “contrarians” love to highlight that the biggest companies in the world don’t always stay the biggest (due to competition, see chart below).

However, considering the current market megatrend themes, the stocks’ ecosystem moats, and the higher earnings growth trajectory, I expect the S&P 493 to continue underperforming the Mag 7 for years into the future. That’s not to say no stock in the S&P 493 will outperform any stock in the Mag 7 (that would be false, there will be select big winners in the S&P 493), however, on average, I’d still prefer to own the Mag 7 over the S&P 493.

Next let’s consider how the Mag 7 stocks rank compared to each other. This is my personal ranking and yours may be very different

*note: I am including 8 stocks in the ranking (including Broadcom (AVGO) which is now larger, by market cap, than several of the other traditional Mag 7 stocks).

8. Apple (AAPL)

Coming in at number eight in my ranking, Apple is one of the bluest of the blue-chip companies, with solid margins, healthy growth (see table above) and an incredible moat (iPhones are loved, customers are loyal and locked into the ecosystem, and the company’s R&D is efficient).

For a little overview, “Products” is Apple’s main business segment with the iPhone/Mac accounting for ~75% revenues and with ~40% of margins. Next is “Services (e.g., App Store/iCloud) accounting for ~25% of revenues, but with an impressive ~70% margin.

From a growth standpoint, Apple is healthy (thanks to ramping AI integration into devices (courtesy of Google) and emerging markets), but Apple’s growth is significantly lower than other Mag 7 stocks (see our earlier table for comparison).

For example, Apple's integration of Apple Intelligence (featuring on-device AI capabilities, enhanced Siri—with major upgrades like Gemini-powered features rolling out in 2026), and privacy-focused tools (across iPhone, iPad, Mac, and other devices) is expected to drive hardware upgrade cycles, boost services adoption, and accelerate revenue growth (by pressuring users to replace older models for full AI functionality).

Further, this AI push, combined with strong expansion in emerging markets (like India, Southeast Asia, Latin America, and the Middle East), is fueling Apple's overall momentum (offsetting softer performance in areas like China) and positioning the company for sustained double-digit growth in key regions.

However, to put that all in perspective, Apple’s expected growth is still lower than other Mag 7 stocks. And Apple has been paying a dividend (with an incredible long-term growth rate) which is a sign of financial health, but also an indication of relatively lower growth (if they had higher growth then reinvesting in their own business would become a higher priority than paying a growing dividend).

Further risks include iPhone revenue dependency, ongoing regulatory scrutiny, and the potential for supply chain disruptions.

Overall, Apple is an impressive blue-chip dividend growth stock, but not as impressive as the other Mag 7 opportunities.

7. Broadcom (AVGO)

Broadcom (basically a semiconductor company) generates ~58% of its revenue from AI/chips (and does so with impressive ~70% margins). It also generates ~42% of its revenue from Software (which is basically VMware) with incredible ~90% margins.

Broadcom competes with Nvidia (ground zero for AI) in the AI infrastructure space but in a complementary way rather than a direct head-to-head manner. Specifically, while Nvidia dominates general-purpose GPUs for AI training and inference (with its unmatched CUDA ecosystem), Broadcom focuses on custom ASICs (e.g., designing TPUs for Google, accelerators for OpenAI and others) and high-performance networking that enable large-scale AI clusters, often as a cost-effective alternative for hyperscalers seeking to reduce dependency on Nvidia's GPUs.

This basically positions Broadcom as a key challenger in inference and custom silicon, with growing deals (e.g., multibillion dollar OpenAI rollout in 2026-2029), but Nvidia retains a strong moat in programmable, high-performance compute (again, making the rivalry more about market share shifts than outright replacement).

And from a growth perspective, Broadcom has an impressive AI backlog, especially considering its VMware integrations and hyperscaler deals. Further, Broadcom has the advantages of: (1) custom AI silicon, (2) high-margin software, and (3) customer lock-ins.

Specifically, Broadcom's high-margin software primarily comes from its Infrastructure Software segment (dominated by VMware products, especially for private/hybrid cloud management).

Further, VMware's growth potential remains healthy with low double-digit revenue increases expected in FY2026 from VMware Cloud Foundation adoption and private cloud/AI workloads, though its Total Addressable Market (“TAM”) in enterprise virtualization/cloud management is mature (~$50-100B+ broader hybrid cloud market), with upside from AI-integrated private clouds amid enterprise on-premises demand.

And from a risk standpoint, Broadcom has significant client concentration, tariff exposure and margin pressures. For example, its top five end customers (primarily hyperscalers for AI/custom chips) accounted for ~40% of net revenue in FY2025 (per 10-K), and heavy reliance on just five major AI clients, increasing vulnerability to demand shifts or spending cuts.

Tariff exposure also poses a risk considering ~20% of its revenue is tied to China, exposing the company to U.S.-China trade tensions and potential retaliatory tariffs (though diversification efforts, such as partnerships in other regions, mitigate some impact).

Margin pressure is also a risk and arises from a growing mix of lower-margin AI system-level sales (including third-party components), with Q1 FY2026 gross margins expected to dip 100 basis points and trends potentially continuing throughout 2026 as AI revenue scales, despite high software margins (93%) providing an offset.

Overall, Broadcom is a massive juggernaut in the right place at the right time (cloud and AI megatrend beneficiary), but its TAM is limited, margins face pressures, and its overall earnings growth rate is expected to be lower than other Mag 7 stocks.

6. Amazon (AMZN)

Amazon is known for its online products marketplace (which generates 60% of the company’s total revenue from North America and another 25% from International), however this is low margin business, and the real profits come from Amazon Web Services (i.e. its cloud business) which generates only ~15% of total revenues, but does so with an impressive ~35% margin.

Amazon’s big competitive advantages are its enormous e-commerce scale and its AWS cloud moat, as well as its logistics network.

Amazon growth will come from several areas. For example, AWS AI workloads give the company a massive backlog (monetization ahead). Also, high-margin advertising revenue is expected to ramp (projected to exceed $70-80B annually with 20%+ growth from AI targeting and retail media). Further, Amazon has retail efficiency opportunities from automation/robotics and emerging AI agents. All of these position Amazon for earnings leverage, valuation re-rating, and potential outperformance in 2026 (particularly as AI investments begin yielding returns).

For some color, Amazon's robotics and automation efforts in fulfillment centers (e.g., deploying over 1 million robots coordinated by AI models like DeepFleet) are projected to drive significant cost savings (e.g., potentially $7.5 billion annually by 2026 through reduced manual labor and efficiency gains like 25% faster processing), and thereby contributing to retail margin expansion and operating leverage.

However, Amazon's shares have underperformed other Magnificent 7 stocks over the last year (2025) primarily due to heavy AI infrastructure capital expenditures (contributing to a plunge in free cash flow), slower-than-expected AWS growth (relative to rivals like Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud), tariff uncertainties (impacting retail operations), and an overall lack of fresh narratives for investors to get excited about (after prior themes like cost cuts and AWS recovery played out), and resulting in single-digit gains for Amazon (while peers like Alphabet and Nvidia soared in 2025).

Worth mentioning, from a risk standpoint, Amazon does face retail competition (Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), Mercado Libre (MELI), to name a few). Also, antitrust probes and tariff impacts could weigh down the company.

Overall, Amazon has plenty of growth and catalysts, and the shares could be due for a re-rating higher, especially as they have lagged other Mag 7 AI leaders.

5. Alphabet (GOOGL)

As described earlier, and despite the very strong rally for the shares over the last year, Alphabet is still priced reasonably considering its leadership in cloud, dominance in advertising, AI growth trajectory and powerful competitive advantages.

4. Tesla (TSLA)

Perhaps most controversial of the Mag 7 (and not just because of the political forays of its leader (Elon Musk) into government efficiency and taking over social media platform Twitter/X), is Tesla. The volatility is huge as bulls and bears battle over valuing the current business (for example, as a car company it is extraordinarily overvalued) and a future business (the TAM of robotaxis and robots is enormous and excites many growth investors).

For some perspective, Tesla’s Automotive segment (electric vehicles) drives 80% of revenues (and with ~17% margins), while Energy/Storage drives 10% of revenue (and with 26% margins), and Services drives 10% of revenues (with ~6% margins).

However, it is Tesla's Full Self-Driving ("FSD") autonomy software, which relies on extensive real-world data training and over-the-air updates, positions the company as a leader in unsupervised self-driving technology (with plans for robotaxi deployments and broader AI integration in 2026). This is what is getting growth investors excited, and could lead to more big gains for the shares.

Tesla is currently trading at premium valuations (for example 13.8x forward sales) as investors bet heavily that FSD could add potentially add massive market value (through recurring subscriptions, robotaxi services, and autonomous market dominance) despite the execution risks.

Further, Tesla's Optimus humanoid robots (designed for tasks in factories, homes, and beyond) represent another pivotal shift in the company's strategy toward AI-driven robotics, with production ramping up in 2026 (for example, aiming for millions of units annually at a targeted price of $20,000).

This robotics initiative is significantly driving Tesla's current elevated valuation as investors bet on Elon Musk's vision that Optimus could comprise the majority of the company's future value through massive revenue from automation (although execution risks in this "prove-it" year could lead to more volatility).

Tesla’s advantages basically stem from its electric vehicle tech lead, its supercharger network, its CEO and arguably its “brand charisma.”

Risks for Tesla include EV competition and margin erosion, production delays (execution risk), regulatory risks and key man risk (Elon Musk can be a bit of a loose cannon at times).

3. Microsoft (MSFT)

If you are trying to optimize risk-versus-reward, a portfolio of stocks is typically much better than betting big on a single name, but if you had to bet on a single name, the pure combination of growth, profits and stability of Microsoft’s business (it’s one of only two companies with a triple-A credit rating—higher than the U.S. government) makes Microsoft the opposite of Tesla in terms of speculative risk (i.e., Microsoft is a brilliant combination of steady, profitable, innovative, high growth).

For perspective, Microsoft generates ~40% of its revenue from its Productivity segment (Office products) and with extremely high margins. Next ~38% of revenue comes from its Cloud (Azure) business, also with strong (and improving) margins. Finally, Personal Computing (Windows/Xbox) contribute ~22% of revenue, albeit with a bit lower margins).

Microsoft’s competitive advantages stem from its expanding AI/cloud leadership position, its integrated ecosystem, and its very high recurring revenue model.

Growth will come from initiatives like accelerating Azure AI adoption (with 40% revenue growth and a $155B+ backlog), rapid Copilot enterprise scaling, Microsoft 365 price hikes (of ~16% in mid-2026), and truly massive AI infrastructure investments (including a $250B OpenAI deal through 2030 that could fuel a rebound by adding big additional revenue and proving monetization potential).

Despite the company’s powerful growth trajectory, Microsoft's shares underperformed other Mag 7 stocks in recent months (late 2025 to early 2026) primarily due to investor concerns over escalating AI capital expenditures (i.e. reaching a record $35 billion in Q1 FY2026) which have eroded free cash flow and raised questions about near-term returns on AI investments. And this is compounded by Alphabet's Gemini/AI advancements (pressuring Microsoft's OpenAI partnership and broader Mag 7 valuation scrutiny), especially amid Microsoft’s slowing profit growth projections of 18% for 2026 (still quite high though!).

Specific risks include intense cloud competition (although there is room for multiple winners), antitrust regulations and cybersecurity threats (both real and reputational).

Nonetheless, all things considered, Microsoft is a dramatically more attractive business than most S&P 500 stocks, and that includes many of the Mag 7.

2. Meta (META)

Meta spent big on AI capex, and the market has punished the shares as a result. However, this is likely a short-term phenomenon (as Meta has experienced many times in the past) as CEO Mark Zuckerberg focuses on long-term growth rather than appeasing short-term Wall Street preferences.

For perspective, Meta’s main business segment (advertising from its Family of Apps) contributes ~98% of total revenues and with an incredible ~30%+ net margin). Reality Labs (Virtual Reality) contributes ~2% of revenues (and generates profit losses).

Meta’s main competitive advantage is its astounding 3.5B+ users, enabling highly-targeted ads and powerful network effects.

Looking forward, Meta growth will come from AI advertising tools, monetization of messaging and impressive user engagement.

In particular, growth initiatives (that could fuel a rebound relative to Mag 7 peers) include accelerating AI-powered advertising tools (like Advantage+ driving $60B+ annualized run-rate and 26%+ ad revenue growth), successful monetization of WhatsApp/Threads (through AI integrations and new channels), continued strong core ad momentum and potential cost discipline (e.g., Reality Labs cuts), and especially demonstrating profitable AI execution, thereby positioning Meta's valuation (~21x forward earnings) as attractive (and for upside as sentiment shifts toward proven monetization).

Risks for Meta continue to include privacy regulations, advertising volatility (that ebbs ad flows with the economy) and metaverse losses.

As alluded to, Meta shares have underperformed other Mag 7 stocks in recent months (late 2025 into early 2026) primarily due to investor concerns over massive capital expenditures tied to aggressive AI infrastructure and data center builds (which have pressured free cash flow, sparked fear of delayed returns on investment, and triggered a post-earnings sell-off as shares are still down ~17% from August 2025 highs).

Overall, however, these selloffs are par for the course for Meta, as CEO Mark Zuckerberg continues to focus on long-term growth rather than short-term market expectations.

1. Nvidia (NVDA)

Nvidia is ground zero for the great datacenter cloud migration and now the AI revolution as surging demand for training and inference in generative AI clears a continuing path for massive high growth. And what makes this opportunity particularly spectacular (besides its already massively impressive scale) is the shares remain relatively inexpensive (particularly after the share price has been sideways in recent months).

For starters, Nvidia’s Data Center Segment has grown to 90% of total revenues with insane 75% margins. It’s once dominant gaming segment generates 8% of revenues (small only relative to Data Center) and with lower margins.

From a competitive advantage standpoint, Nvidia’s CUDA software creates an extremely powerful moat (as the industry had been trained over nearly two decades to use it, and switching costs for users are high (steep/long learning curve). And the network effects (as AI grows) makes it even harder for competitors (such as AMD) to overcome (even as hardware alternatives emerge).

From a growth standpoint, Nvidia's TAM in AI-driven data center semiconductors (and related infrastructure) is truly massive and rapidly expanding. Estimates for the broader AI chip and data center market are in the hundreds of billions annually, and with projections for $400B+ by 2027 for data center AI chips. Nvidia basically has a $3-4 trillion multi-year AI infrastructure opportunity (fueled by surging demand for training and inference in generative AI, while its core data center segment alone drives explosive growth amid the ongoing AI megatrend supercycle).

The main risks for Nvidia are basically: (1) the AI megatrend/ supercycle is overblown (which seems unlikely based on hyperscaler capex), (2) the company’s main customers have already overspent, and (3) supply shortages (which can disrupt and delay deliveries).

From a customer concentration standpoint, Nvidia does disclose that two anonymous clients drive ~39% of total revenue:

“Customer A” accounts for 23% of total revenue, and is likely Foxconn/Hon Hai Precision, due to its dominant scale (projected as the world's top server vendor in 2025 by Omdia), close Nvidia partnerships (e.g., Blackwell GPU manufacturing), and explosive AI server growth (up 17% profit in Q3 2025 from Nvidia-related demand). Foxconn basically assembles ~24% of Nvidia GPU servers historically and leads in Asia's data centers.

“Customer B” accounts for 16% of revenue, and is likely Quanta Computer (or its QCT arm), as a key ODM with ~15-22% historical share of Nvidia servers, 100%+ revenue growth in AI-optimized systems in 2025, and deep integration for GB200/Blackwell racks.

From a valuation standpoint, Nvidia still trades at only ~24x forward earnings, which is quite inexpensive considering revenue is growing at over 50% per year, earnings are expected to grow at a rate of over 30% for the next five years, and the company’s forward PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) is only 1.0x (more attractive than other Mag 7 stocks).

Overall, despite Nvidia’s impressive rally over the last decade, it continues to have massive room to run and the shares trade at a very reasonable price. Considering it is now such a large part of the entire US stock market (more than 6%), it would be risky to not own any Nvidia, and it would be prudent to own a significant amount of it in your portfolio—depending, of course, on your own personal situation and goals.

The Bottom Line

U.S. stocks have been on a multi-year rally of strong returns, and there is certainly no guarantee that 2026 will be the same. However, over the long-term, earnings is expected to continue to grow for the Magnificent 7 (and at a faster pace than the S&P 493) and that bodes well for stock prices (particularly those trading at reasonable share prices).

Some Mag 7 stocks have recently performed better than others (such as Alphabet), but that doesn’t mean you need to sell them (that would be like benching Michael Jordan because he just won the MVP), especially considering valuations remain quite reasonable relative to growth.

Other Mag 7 stocks (Nvidia, Meta and Microsoft) have not performed as well (in terms of share price gains) in recent months, but their business have performed well (despite short term concerns over heavy capex and noise in general). The reality is these stocks continue to benefit from massive long-term megatrends and market supercycles (as described in this report).

I am not saying every Mag 7 stock will outperform every S&P 493 stock (there will be some extraordinarily impressive returns from some S&P 493 stocks). But on average, I do expect the Mag 7 to keep outperforming the S&P 493, and some Mag 7 stocks will likely perform better than others (as described in this report). I have positioned my own personal investment portfolio (i.e., The BH Disciplined Growth Portfolio) accordingly (and may make some small-ish tweaks in the coming days.

Overall, disciplined, goal-focused, long-term investing continues to be a winning strategy. And you need to be smart and do what is right for you.

data.title: Rivian: I Covered My Short Position Following 27% Drop In One Month (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Rivian Automotive (RIVN) stock plunged in the last month following my article, Rivian Needs More Capital: Rating Downgrade. In that article, I discussed Rivian's capital needs for the upcoming R2 production ramp, included a detailed scenario analysis, and concluded as my base-case scenario that Rivian would continue as a going concern but was likely to dilute existing investors.

Since then, Rivian stock rapidly declined by 27 percent in one month, which is rare even for a volatile stock like Rivian, underperforming the 0.54 percent rise in the S&P 500 index, the 6.25 percent rise in the Russell 2000 index, and the 8.15 percent decline in Tesla (TSLA) stock:

Following this rapid drop, I sold my near-dated Rivian puts, and in the next section, I discuss the reasons why I did, despite expecting further downside in the stock in the next two years.

Why Did I Sell My Rivian Puts?

In my last article, I valued Rivian at 1x book value as my base case, or $5 per share, which is still 69 percent lower than Friday's closing price of $15.95 per share. Having said that, however, my article also included two other scenarios: a Restructuring/Liquidation scenario and a Bull Case. For your convenience, I included the following quote from my last article on the three scenarios:

Rivian's cash buffer allows the company three scenarios:

  1. Base Case, 60% probability: Rivian continues as a going concern in 2026, in line with the outlook for the company that I discussed above. Rivian ramps R2 production slower than expected and needs to tap markets for $3B to $5B of equity capital, diluting existing investors by 15% to 20%, given Rivian's $26B current market capitalization.
  2. Restructuring or Liquidation, 35% probability: R2 production ramp proves challenging and/or a macro slowdown stresses capital markets. Rivian cannot raise additional capital in 2026, and its assets are liquidated in an orderly fashion with reasonable recovery rates in 2027 or 2028.
  3. Bull Case, 5% probability: Rivian ramps R2 production rapidly and profitably, R2 demand proves greater than I estimate, and operating leverage materializes.

Given the 27 percent plunge, the stock now prices materially less optimistic scenarios than what I discussed above. Further, I recognize the fact that Rivian already produced validation units of R2 ahead of my base-case timeline, and if that continues, it could invalidate my base case for the stock. The combination of these two factors made me reconsider my aggressive position on the stock.

I next discuss two reasons why Rivian's downside may be limited in 2026.

R2 "Option Value"

Rivian's equity carries a significant "option value" tied to the R2 program and future demand: If Rivian executes well and translates management's and retail investors' unrelenting optimism about the vehicle into a rapid and profitable production ramp, then it could raise more capital to extend its runway.

My base case is that Rivian will eventually find R2 demand is not as high as planned, but I estimate that the market is currently pricing the R2 option value somewhere between $5 and $10 per share, on top of the company's $5 per share of book value, intangible assets such as brand value and intellectual property, as well as its growing software/services segment.

I believe that until market participants are convinced about the eventual success or failure of the R2 program, the market will continue to assign some option value to the R2 program, and I don't think the market will be convinced one way or another until the production is ramped up by early 2027.

Liquidity

On September 30, 2025, Rivian had $4.441 billion of cash & cash equivalents, $2.647 billion of short-term investments, and $598 million available under its $1.5 billion ABL revolving facility, or a total liquidity of $7.686 billion. In addition, the company disclosed in Form 10-Q that it secured a $6 billion DOE loan, subject to certain conditions such as positive gross margin and certain sales targets. Finally, the company could receive an additional $1 billion from Volkswagen Group in October 2026. The following section from the latest Form 10-Q provides the details of Rivian's off-balance sheet financing arrangements:

In other words, if Rivian ramps R2 production and sales to volumes sufficient to achieve positive gross margin, it will unlock ample liquidity to build its next product, the R3. My base case is that Rivian will not be able to sell the product at volumes needed to achieve a sustainable positive gross margin, but I respect the possibility that my forecast may be wrong and that there is a significant option value assigned to the R2 program. If Rivian achieves a positive gross margin with R2, then the stock could surge due to the following reason.

High Short Interest

12.51 percent of Rivian's outstanding shares are currently short with 4 Days to Cover, which sets up a possible short squeeze in the coming months.

Even if the R2 production ramp stalls due to limited demand for the product, as I expect, I don't expect the stock to decline below $10 per share in 2026 due to the company's high liquidity. At $15.92 per share, it didn't make sense for me to carry the risk of a large short squeeze in 2026 only for another $5 to $6 per share of incremental return on my bear position.

Watch For This

Rivian will announce its financial results for Q4 2025 on February 12, 2026, after market close. I expect management to affirm that customer deliveries will begin in Q2 2026, possibly earlier than the expected June 2026 timeline. This alone could ignite a bounce in the stock following its recent plunge.

Further, if management also states that Rivian is targeting a positive gross margin in 2027 with R2 production ramped to volume, then I would not want to be short this volatile stock with high short interest in the ensuing days.

Bottom Line

Despite maintaining a bearish view for the next 1-2 years, I exited my near-dated puts following the rare plunge because any upbeat management commentary around production and gross margin targets on the earnings call, combined with the high short interest in the stock, sets up unfavorable risk/reward for bears in the near term. I maintain my $5 price target, discussed in my last article, and upgrade the stock from Strong Sell to Sell.

data.title: January Ifo Index Pours Cold Water On Hopes Of A Stellar Rebound For The German Economy
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

This morning, Germany has literally slid into weather and traffic chaos. While this chaos will only be temporary, the just-released Ifo index suggests that leaving the economic chaos behind will be more cumbersome than seeing today’s snow melt away.

In January, Germany’s leading indicator, the Ifo index, remained unchanged at 87.6. While the current assessment improved somewhat, expectations weakened. The reading should be taken with a pinch of salt, as it is unclear whether most participants sent in their answers before or after US President Donald Trump’s decision not to impose additional tariffs on several European countries.

More generally speaking, the unchanged Ifo index reflects the uncertainty that has hit the German economy again on the back of geopolitical tensions and tariff threats.

Time to get more positive about Germany

Despite this morning's disappointing Ifo index reading, there are good reasons to be more positive about the German economy. The latest macro data indicates a clear turning point in industry at the end of last year. Industrial orders have now increased for three consecutive months, and even the argument that bulk orders drove the November surge does not really concern us; with the fiscal spending programme, more of these bulk orders will come this year. In fact, bulk orders could be the new normal, not the exception.

While industry is going through a soft and tentative period of cyclical turning, admittedly at still shockingly weak levels, the announced infrastructure and defence investment plans should finally begin to reach the economy this year. Critics often overlook the sluggishness of Germany’s federal decision-making process. It took until late last year for parliament to approve the 2026 budget and almost 30 military procurement contracts.

With the rapid expansion of defence production capacity, there is a good chance that a large part of defence spending will remain within the domestic economy and not leak to other countries. We see a clearly higher multiplier for defence investments than anticipated a year ago. Last but not least, even though corporates never tire of complaining about elevated energy costs, the government’s decision to bring those costs down to one-third of their current levels should bring further relief, if implemented.

Biggest risk: from national depression to national complacency

Even if today's Ifo index undermines some of our optimism, an economic liftoff is clearly in the making. The Ifo index reading, however, does reflect the uncertainty that has hit the German economy again on the back of geopolitical tensions and tariff threats.

As much as we remain convinced of the upcoming economic recovery, we continue to stress that the economy’s problems are deeply rooted, often structural and largely self-made, except for the China problem. Solving these issues quickly is impossible. This is a completely different challenge from some 20 years ago, when Germany was the ‘sick man of Europe’. Ongoing geopolitical tensions and the everlasting threat and uncertainty regarding US tariffs are further complicating factors for Germany’s economic outlook.

Despite any cyclical upswing, the economy still needs an almost complete makeover, ranging from well-known measures such as reducing red tape and introducing e-government to mastering and lowering the financial burden of demographics or through tax cuts. It is up to German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and his government to implement these reforms this year and turn a long-awaited rebound into a sustainable recovery.

The too-long period of de facto stagnation seems to be coming to an end. However, the list of potential downside risks is still long. And while there is very little Germany can do to prevent new external shocks, it has full and absolute control over not shifting from one extreme to the other: the biggest domestic risk remains any sudden shift from national depression to national complacency.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Nucor: 15% Upside Potential Driven By Investment Completion And Strategic Growth
data.description:
Executive Summary

Nucor (NUE) is a perfect choice for investors seeking a position in the US steel sector due to its operational excellence. We are positive on the stock both for the sector's growth outlook (+2.2% cagr in the period 2024-2030) and for company-specific reasons, such as the positive impact of the last few years investments on earnings from 2026.

Therefore, we have a buy rating with a $207 target price, based on our dcf model. It Implies a 15% upside potential from January 21, 2026, closing price of $ 180.23.

Company Description

Nucor is the largest and most diversified American steel and steel products manufacturer. The company's operations are heavily concentrated in the US, where it generates 98% of its revenues.

With regards to segment breakdown, the Steel Mills segment (production of sheet bars, structural steel, and plates) generates 62% of revenues, the Steel Products segment (joists, decking, fasteners, and tubing) accounted for 33% and the Raw materials segment the remaining 5%.

The company's strength is its use of electric arc furnace (EAF) technology, which offers much more flexible production management. Indeed, unlike traditional blast furnaces, which must operate continuously for years (because shutting them down would cause significant structural damage), EAFs can be shut down and restarted quickly based on market demand or energy costs. Furthermore, electric technology allows for much more precise and faster temperature control (up to 3000°C), facilitating the production of special steels and high-quality alloys.

Nucor has outperformed both the S&P 500 (total return of 47% versus 16%) and the S&P Materials sector index (8.4%) over the last 12 months. In our view, the outperformance is attributable to two elements:

1) The Trump administration's increase in US steel imports tariffs from 25% to 50% for most countries, except for Europe, which has a 15% tariff rate.

2) The release of better-than-expected quarterly results. In Q3 2025, the company posted revenues of $8.52 billion, exceeding consensus estimates of $8.22 billion. Ebitda reached $1.3 billion, and eps was $2.63, up 18.5% versus the consensus estimate of $2.22. The Steel Products segment led profit growth thanks to strong demand for rebar and data center products, while the Steel Mills segment saw a decline in the latter due to lower profit margins.

Investment Case

We think that Nucor could benefit over the next few quarters from several favorable elements:

1. Steel demand in the US should grow at a healthy pace in the coming years led by a recovery in the manufacturing and non-residential construction sectors for the expansionary fiscal and monetary policies. Investments in data centers and the electricity distribution network, necessary for AI development, could also have a significant impact on US steel demand. According to several research institutions, steel demand could grow at a 2.2% cagr between 2024 and 2030.

2. The last few years capex made to increase production and existing plants more efficient should begin to bear fruit during 2026. At the time of publishing the Q3 2025 results, the management announced that 80% of the $10 billion investment program had been completed. In this scenario, the next few years results should benefit from both the impact of these investments and the capex reduction.

3. The order backlog remains significant at 3.5 million tons (+30% year-on-year).

4. The company's valuation (see section below) remains attractive despite the recent stock price increase. Based on our Unlevered dcf model and multiple analysis, we estimate a fair value between $207, with a 15% upside potential from January 21, 2026, closing price of $180.23.

What Could Go Wrong

The main risk to our positive stance on Nucor is a US investment slowdown. The manufacturing and non-residential construction sectors could suffer from weak growth if the Fed's recent round of rate cuts prove insufficient to revive economic activity. Moreover, the data center sector could suffer should the enthusiasm for ai development wanes. Other factors that could negatively impact the results include an increase in electricity prices or potential delays in the full operation of the West Virginia plant and other facilities in which the company has invested, delaying the impact on cash flow.

Valuation

We value Nucor using both a dcf model and multiple analysis. Our dcf assumptions include:

  • Ebit growing at 7.4% cagr in the period 2024-2030 as the Company could benefit from the last few years’ capex to increase operating margins.
  • Capex peaking in 2025 and 2026 as the $10 billion capex plan is completed.
  • Wacc of 8.0%.
  • A terminal growth rate of 2.0%, in line with the projected potential real long-term growth rate of global economy.

This yields a of $207 target Price, implying a 15% upside potential from January 21, 2026, closing price.

A multiple analysis highlights a less favorable picture on the stock. The stock is trading at an estimated 2026 P/E of 15.4x above the 2016-2025 average of 13.7x. The P/E would return below the average in 2027 (13.1x). However, at our target price calculated with the dcf model, the stock would trade at a 2026 P/E of 17.7x, well above the long-term average but below the 2025 P/E (21.9x). However, we believe that better earning perspectives for the coming years and the recent Company’s focus on higher value-added products, as indicated by the growing share of the Steel Products segment on total revenue (from 24% in 2021 to 33% in 9M ’25) deserve higher multiple valuation.

The View from the Street

Analysts covering the stock at major investment firms have a positive view on Nucor: twelve analysts have a buy rating, three a hols rating, and none have a sell rating.

Despite the positive opinion on the stock, the consensus target price of $182.38 is in line with current prices, well below our target price ($207). However, we believe it could be revised upward in the coming months, in line with what happened in the 2021-2024 period.

Conclusion

Despite having already shown a solid upward trend over the last 12 months for the better-than-expected results and the Trump administration's increased tariffs on steel imports, we believe Nucor still has an interesting upside potential. The stock should benefit from strong US steel demand, confirmed by the order backlog, and the positive impact of the completion of the $10 billion investment plan implemented in recent years. We assign a buy rating on Nucor stock with a $207 target price.

data.title: Siemens: A Case To Be Made For The Macro-Related Downside
data.description:
Going contrarian against a solid, €200B market cap company is not exactly an easy thing. This is especially true when the company in question is one of very few global AA-rated conglomerates. It's further very difficult when that company is superbly managed and owns significant or majority stakes in companies that you (or I, in this case) love owning and also own stakes in.

But there comes a point when despite a company's theoretical upside in the case of further growth, there's a downside risk so significant and out of the company's control that investing in the business is simply not a good idea.

Siemens (SIEGY) is currently in that position - and I don't foresee that changing.

My last coverage on Siemens was about 2-3 months ago, but I have been covering the stock for as long as I have been writing on Seeking Alpha. In my early career, I owned over 8% of my total portfolio in the company - and part of me is obviously sad, given the returns since, that I did not keep that. At the same time, my overall portfolio performance has been good or fair even compared to the solid return that Siemens has generated.

In this article, I will be offering assumptions and forecasting for Siemens for 2026-2028E, and why I believe the forecasted double-digit EPS growth is unlikely to materialize, or at the very least, too risky to base an investment thesis on. I will argue that there are better alternatives, even within the Siemens 'sphere of influence' and that you could at the very least consider these.

What I am arguing here is essentially the following: It's not that Siemens is a bad business or unlikely to grow - it's that further growth at the pace that's being forecasted, which is justifying this company's significant premium, is unlikely given the secular weaknesses in certain end markets as well as certain mixes of macro trends.

Now let me show you what I mean with this.

Siemens - Upside here is unlikely given results, but more importantly, volatile end markets and macro trends

I won't spend much time on going through the qualities of Siemens here, the positive ones. These are all well-established, and I focus on them in previous articles. What I instead will show you is why the company is unlikely to see further significant growth.

Siemens, as it remains today, is a mix of attractive business areas, but one of those business areas is the Digital Industries, or DI segment. This focuses on things like automation and digitization of processes, specifically for companies in manufacturing. Siemens is a full-service provider, offering both hardware solutions in EDA as well as software solutions for this, covering the spectrum from industrial automation to digital transformation. Major end-users and markets for this include automotive, aerospace and various machining and construction businesses.

Recent results in the company's reports have focused in part on a slowdown of growth in the DI segment. As tends to be the case with explosive growth segments or areas like this, there comes a time when high growth turns to low growth. Following the post-Covid-19 period, there were long years of very high demand for these services. But trends not only out of Siemens but peers as well suggest that customers are destocking existing inventories in automation and digitalization in a variety of areas. This impacts the short-cycle results and trends in the business. And while this for now has been disguised a bit by upside in Siemens Energy (SMEGF) and other things, the signs are there for anyone wanting to look at them, including the company cutting jobs.

These signs include weak demand from both Germany and China, which for Siemens are key geographies, as well as an increased competition and peer presence. While Siemens is a market-leader here, it's not immune to the margin pressure that arises when new players enter the market, and that's what's happening here. Furthermore, competition for this is not just coming from smaller, more agile players, but Siemens-like peers like ABB (ABB), Schneider (SBGSY), and Rockwell (ROK). The coming of AI has also enabled startups focused entirely on this. It's not incorrect, therefore, to call the space more crowded than before. The barriers to entry have been levelled thanks to no more requirements for coding proficiency, with tools that enable both low- and no-code solutions.

While this is no end to Siemens DI, it impacts the segment and the business, and that is my argument. The company still has a large backlog here, but I argue it's disguising trends that over the coming 2-5 years will not be as favorable as analysts are claiming that they will.

Aside from competition in DI, we can look at the rise of increased competition in most of the areas where Siemens was/is expecting significant and above-average growth. The issue is that even if Mobility and Electrification are posting good results, these are lower-margin segments compared to automation - and if automation and DI margins collapse or go lower, then this would impact Siemens as a whole. And the expectations for both Mobility and Electrification are down as well - not just from me, but from other analysts and the market, such as Erste Group, which in 2025 clarified its position.

The thing is, even if the segments performed as expected, or at relatively high levels, you'd be investing in Siemens at a valuation that by all accounts is extremely stretched. I will go into it more in the valuation section, but the fact is that has only traded at this level at one time the past 20 years - right before the GFC. This is not a favorable outlook by any historical or forward-looking measure.

I'm not forecasting another GFC, or GFC-like crash, where Siemens went from a peak of €95/share, which then was 25-26x P/E, to less than €38/share about 1-2 years later, but I am saying that analysts would be very wise to consider the current levels of valuation, what they imply, and the current levels of return and what those imply.

The implications for these, when put in context with things like the DI performance, the underlying automation, electrification, mobility, AI and other markets (and trends in those markets), do not "gel" all that well with an even higher share price. Frankly, even if the company fulfilled all of its targets and expectations during the next 2-3 years, I would view the company as even more inflated than before.

Siemens - Upside for the company based on valuation and expectations

One thing you have to understand about me and how I invest is the following: It's far more important to me to avoid losses compared to securing market-beating gains.

That means that I will go out of my way to avoid investments that I view as higher risk or that might lower my overall capital, even if those investments, according to some, have a high likelihood of success.

This sort of strategy is suited for the risk-averse, the older investor, the person who's "made their nut", and doesn't want to lose it, even at the cost of somewhat lower returns or "missing out" on some things.

So know this going in - if you're the type of more risk-tolerant and even risk-seeking investor, then you'll need to consider my targets and assumptions with this in mind. If I was more risk-taking and accepting of higher targets, my PT for Siemens would look different. A bullish investor might look at Siemens and give the company a 25-26x P/E on a forward basis, despite the fact that the company has never averaged this in 20+ years, or that even at 26x P/E, your RoR would be only barely about 16.5% per year.

It would also ignore that the 20-year average is closer to 17x P/E, and the highest average is about 20x, with longer-term averages implying a RoR close to negative (or below 2% per year, inclusive of the yield, meaning negative without the company's dividend). That means that a savings account is giving you better RoR than Siemens forecasted at a conservative valuation with these assumptions.

That is not a particularly favorable situation to "enter" into, no matter how solid the fundamentals or the prospects might be, in your estimate.

Consider also, for a moment, the fact that Siemens AG retains a 35%+ stake in Siemens Energy, which has been on an absolute tear for about 1 year. That means that at least part of the reason that Siemens is up the performance of Siemens Energy. Conversely, if you believe that SMEGF has gone up too far, too quickly, you should consider the impacts this might have on Siemens as well. This is my stance.

Siemens is more and more a pure play on automation. This is further confirmed by the company's recent M&As, which all to some extent focus on automation and DI. It does not make the other businesses bad, but it underscores the problems that could arise if a secular underperformance in DI was to come.

I forecast no more than a 4-5% longer-term AEPS growth because I believe DI and smart infrastructure will grow lower than expected. I don't expect a cyclical recovery in DI to post-COVID-19 trends, but I believe this spike to have been a one-time thing. The order backlog (extensive) does not change my thesis. I assume that the electrification trend that's ongoing will be less than expected globally, and with a longer-term industrial profit margin of 15-16% at most, I come to a 17x forward P/E, the 15-20 year average. Most analysts who forecast the company higher, at say €250-€300/share, assume 17-19% margins and a significant tailwind from DI recovery.

To me, this implies a €250/share fair value, not a PT - the PT, risks included, is closer to €175/share - this was my share price target in my last article, and I'm doubling down on it here.

Risks to the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Risks for Siemens include, aside from potential margin pressures and the risks mentioned in this article not only to DI and to Siemens Energy as a part of the thesis, a dilution from its diversified portfolio relative to AI.

It remains unfavorable when you compare it to straight-electrical equipment manufacturers among its peers, like Schneider or Legrand, both of which I cover (and one of which I invest in).

As mentioned above, Siemens' strategy at this point of its growth trajectory is mostly based on inorganic growth, which obviously comes with the risk of overpaying for assets (which I believe the company is already doing, such as when it bought Altair for over $10B at over 30x P/E, which is almost twice Siemens own valuation over time).

Aside from that, its portfolio structure needs to be considered. The issues in Energy were clearly ones that showed us how they can affect the holding company's business and valuation.

Thesis

Siemens is a beyond-solid company that I've covered for years and years. Valuation, or the premiumization of this, however, means that I only maintain a small coverage position of below 0.1% as of this update in 2026. I believe valuation shows us, together with forecasts, why we should not be buying at this valuation, and I believe the risks related to DI and automation are very significant here.

At the right price, Siemens Aktiengesellschaft becomes a "BUY" strong enough to make me ignore or put second most other investments, and I would then put 1-3% of my total capital to work to get exposure here. But this would require a 30-40% drop from the levels we see in January.

My PT (price target) for Siemens remains at €175, updated for the 2026E period, even with the positive estimates for growth. I consider these estimates to be too exuberant to be trusted as a basis for investing, and therefore rate Siemens a "HOLD".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of €175/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apple: Q1 Results Could Trigger A Price Reversal, But Buy Regardless
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) is down by over 10 percent from when my last bullish article on the name was published. In that piece, I expanded on an earlier thesis about the generational demand pull for iPhones in general, but made more specific by iPhone 17 demand being a pull-forward force for FY 2026.

Now that we are approaching Q1 2026 earnings after the market closes this Thursday, the 29th, it seems to be a good time to let investors know whether the decline from the recent peak in early December 2025 was justified, or if there is an opportunity here to take advantage of the market's inevitable reaction next week.

I do expect some price action at earnings, but will it be to the upside or will we see a continuance of the current decline to even farther below $250? That is what I hope to answer today, to the best of my ability.

What Triggered the Decline?

Our first angle of discovery should be the stock price decline itself, to see whether it was a reflection of larger market dynamics or just specific to AAPL.

There does not seem to be any evidence of a broad market impact on AAPL stock. On December 2, the stock was far ahead of not just the S&P 500 (SP500) but also the top two sectors of the past year - tech and communications.

Clearly, this is not a sectoral or broad-market phenomenon. I knew it was not, but I had to be sure to rule it out.

So, what did trigger the early December decline?

In reality, the indicators of a potential decline came in two weeks before that, when the Financial Times reported that the Cupertino smartphone behemoth was "stepping up its succession planning efforts, as it prepares for Tim Cook to step down as chief executive as soon as next year." Shortly after this report, the stock did drop, but quickly recovered to scale to the December 2 high.

While this somewhat weakened the sentiment around the stock, the real shocker came in early December - the management "shakeup" reported that week. Among those exiting was the senior VP of ML and AI Strategy, John Giannandrea.

In and of itself, that would hardly have been the reason to trigger a sell-off, but the sudden departure of several senior members of management following on from the news of Cook's reported exit in 2026 was a little too much for the market to handle in my view.

There were - and are - several other developments that contributed to the momentum and the longevity of the decline, but the implications of these news items is likely to have caused at least a small minority of investors to sell off. That makes sense to me because AAPL was trading near all-time highs. This is when the less serious bulls tend to take profits off the table or resize their positions to maintain the weighting.

The broader picture here is Apple finally admitting implicitly that its AI ambitions are moving in a different direction. From directly focusing on AI investments, it is pivoting to third-party solutions.

This began long before the January 12 news of a Gemini-infused Siri broke. Just before the middle of November, Bloomberg carried a report about Apple's lack of aggressive spending on AI CapEx. More recently, it was reported that Craig Federighi initiated a shift in AI strategy as early as last fall.

Federighi apparently addressed a joint meeting of Apple's software and AI teams, expressing enthusiasm for closer collaboration while also signaling dissatisfaction with the company's pace of progress in artificial intelligence. Some members of Apple's foundation models team interpreted the remarks as criticism of their work.

In December, Apple moved to consolidate its AI leadership under Federighi, completing a transition that had begun earlier in the year when responsibility for ‌Siri‌ was removed from the AI group and brought under Federighi's software division. In January, Apple announced plans to use Google's Gemini AI models to power future AI upgrades, including an improved version of ‌Siri‌. In Federighi's view, integrating a third-party model would allow Apple to finally ship a revamped ‌Siri‌ later this year after controversially postponing the update in 2025.

It appears that the confluence of these developments was gaining momentum well before the decline began in December, when the management shakeup occurred.

One can actually see this investor sentiment play out from as early as August 2025, when put call ratios started climbing. The ratio of put-call volumes (black line) speaks for itself, peaking at 3.4 in early December. Now why does that time-window sound familiar, I wonder?

Even more telling is the movement of OTM put call ratios, which generally indicate hedging by institutional investors at levels that are significantly above 1. In the case of AAPL, that ratio stood above 2.2 when the stock peaked.

I think that is sufficient evidence that the market was worried about Apple's AI pivot, but I also believe that the market has now digested the information fully, and could be on the verge of price reversal and a fresh rally on the back of Q1 earnings this week. Current put call levels are much more subdued. While ATM puts are still significant higher than calls, the OTM ratio is now comfortably below 1.0.

Why This Should Matter To You

Most analysts would understandably use the current decline to peddle a 'buy the dip' thesis, but that thesis can prove dangerous when one is unaware of what caused the decline in the first place. So, rather than give you a generic pill like 'unjustified sell-off' and simply hit you over the head with Apple's strong fundamentals to validate that stance, I propose to give you a meaningful reason why the decline is not a sign of any kind of deterioration in AAPL's future prospects.

That being said, more than fundamentals, market perception seems to be guiding AAPL's price right now. We have seen that clearly in the analysis above with the put call ratios rising, and while fundamentals provide a sort of valuation floor, it is market sentiment that gives it seasonal volatility around this time of the year, when the market is trying to guess which way the fundamentals will move based on the latest iPhone's success - or lack thereof, as the case may be.

Therefore, it stands to reason that market sentiment is what will guide the price when Q1 2026 earnings are out next week. The market will look at the headline figures, fingers hovering nervously over the virtual buttons to buy, sell or hedge. Once the perception has been formed, the sentiment takes over.

That means we need to begin with a preview of what might come.

What Market Will Be Looking At for Q1

There were several indicators that iPhone 17 demand was very strong, the most important of which was the CEO's projections reported at the end of October, a month into the crucial holiday quarter.

In an interview with Reuters, Cook said he expects iPhone sales in the current, holiday-focused quarter to grow by double digits year over year and Apple's overall revenue to grow 10-12% year over year. Those forecasts for Apple's fiscal first quarter of 2026 beat analyst estimates of iPhone sales rising 9.8% to $75.91 billion and total sales up 6.6% to $132.53 billion, according to data from LSEG.

If those projections are accurate, that alone is sufficient reason to expect the stock to pop at earnings because it will create a surge of positive sentiment.

But why do I think that will happen?

Apple has generally been conservative with guidance, as evidenced by its pristine EPS beat record over the past two years.

In case you missed it, Cook's guidance was for revenue growth, so why am I using earnings to make the point?

My view is that earnings growth is the real focus for Apple, especially at Q1.

The top line is important from a headline perspective, of course, because it reflects the strength or weakness of the iPhone model released for that fiscal year. Earnings, on the other hand, are material to the price of the stock and are more within the company's direct control. SG&A and R&D outlays are carefully expensed to maximize operating profitability, which flows directly into net margins.

Going a step further, I would also add "maximize cash flows" to that statement because it is the consistently strong cash flow generation that keep the share buyback engine running smoothly quarter after quarter, and this is a crucial component of what supports that valuation floor I mentioned above.

What I Shall be Looking At for Q1 2026

Now that we know that it is earnings and cash flows that power investor sentiment around AAPL stock, what can we expect?

The Street's expectations are implicit for the next several years, and optimism is baked into the stock on the sell side. For this quarter, in particular, these analysts are looking at a growth rate of about 11.5 percent at the bottom line, off a revenue growth rate of a similar 11.4 percent.

These optimistic expectations are not out of place considering Cook's guidance from October, but this actually adds on more risk than ever because a miss at these levels would only serve to trigger a further downward momentum in the stock.

Assuming Apple beats on both lines (but more importantly on adjusted EPS), there is still no guarantee of a reversal in sentiment. Remember that AAPL declined in the weeks after its Q4/FY 2025 results came out at the end of October.

I shall attribute this decline to the fact that the Q4 double-beat was marginal at the top (0.21 percent to the upside), and not very strong at the bottom (4.45 percent to the upside).

Far more relevant to my thesis as it stands today ahead of Q1 earnings is the fact that there has been a significant deterioration of market sentiment around AAPL stock.

This is where it gets challenging to defend a Buy recommendation ahead of the Q1 release, but I posit that the stock is very likely to see that reversal at earnings.

My first clue is Apple's forward earnings multiple, which stands right at its three-year average.

One could certainly argue that it is completely logical for any variable to oscillate around its historical average because that is precisely how the average is calculated, but looking beyond the mathematical phenomenon, one sees that the average also appears to be a line of support/resistance for the earnings multiple, depending on whether it is expanding or compressing.

One should also observe that these valuation moves have very little correlation with the actual earnings results. This is better seen on a price chart.

I find it extremely intriguing that, notwithstanding the rare exception, no earnings release has ever triggered a reversal of the stock's price decline. This is despite a string of adjusted EPS beats over the period in question. In other words, none of the bottoms have ever been marked by an earnings announcement.

What I Shall Take Away Ahead of Q1 Results

My takeaway from this analysis is that the stock may continue to decline despite a single or double beat at Q1. Negative sentiment appears to currently overwhelm any market bullishness on AAPL.

On the other hand, if that sentiment is captured as a moment-in-time snapshot, it would appear that the market is leaning slightly toward bullish. January 30 put and call volatility does not appear overly skewed.

So, on the balance, a decline is certainly not off the table, but if the beat at the bottom line is strong enough, it could provide enough momentum for a reversal. At the very least, it will reassure investors that Apple can still keep growing its earnings and cash flows despite the new direction it has chosen to take on the AI front - that of a 'reseller' of others' AI products.

Despite the continued decline into 2026, the market has still not made up its mind about AAPL. That is why its stock price in the week leading to earnings resembles an expectant father furiously pacing up and down the corridors of the maternity ward. He is, after all, not an 'insider'.

This is how I see the short term dynamics: an apprehensive market waiting on a strongly optimistic iPhone 17 performance to offset worries of a further decline. If that decline does continue after Q1 earnings are out, it would indicate that the negative sentiment has yet to play out, and it might be a while before the stock finds its legs again.

From a long-term perspective, however, the negative sentiment around Apple dropping out of the AI race against the other mega-caps is more than balanced out by the possibilities of leveraging its massive and still-growing hardware install base as a 'delivery mechanism' for the best AI utilities in the market.

My Biggest Takeaway

That implies strong services growth over the next several years, and that will continue to bring more stability into the company's fiscal performance year after year.

In summation, while I do see some downside potential in the short term, I would still advise a staggered Buy before and after earnings. An equal split is good enough because the decision is binary but will still benefit you - if the stock continues to decline, you bought half the tranche at a more expensive cost basis but benefit from the lower price post earnings; on the flip side, if stock price sees a clear reversal, you still bought half the tranche at a lower cost basis, and now there is more upside to look forward to.
