It is a clear win-win as I see it, but please remember that the prerequisite to following this recommendation is your personal sentiment on the stock and your long-term view of the company's growth trajectory. I have laid out my long-term bullish outlook over the course of these last three articles, including this one, because I remain convinced of the long-term attractiveness of this stock regardless of short-term price action. Can you say the same? That is the real question here, is it not?

data.title: Rithm Capital: Get Paid Rich Dividends While Waiting For A Valuation Upgrade
data.description:
I previously covered Rithm Capital Corp. (NYSE:RITM) in July 2025, discussing how the mREIT was finally trading near its book value, thanks to the robust performance metrics and the normalizing yield curve in the short-and-long term interest rates, with it naturally reducing the risks of a potential recession.

Thanks to its rich dividend yields, I had rated the stock as a Buy upon a moderate retracement then, despite the potentially prolonged re-rating nearer to its mature alternative asset management peers.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the RITM stock here, thanks to the well covered/secure dividend incomes, the management's laser focus on delivering diversified mREIT/asset management growth opportunities, the speculative upward re-rating in its valuations, and the consequently rich capital appreciation prospects.

This is despite the likely to be lumpy FQ4'25 performance arising from the ongoing Fed rate cut/recent acquisitions and the stock's sideways trading cadence over the past few years.

RITM's Diversification Continues

Since my last Buy rating, RITM's correction has indeed occurred to the $10.60s before rallying to the $11.70s by the time of writing, with a similar recovery also observed in its mREIT peers in varying degrees.

Part of the pullback in September 2025 may be attributed to the issuance of preferred stocks, including Series E and Series F, along with the $750M of capital raise, as the management aggressively raise cash to fund its recent acquisition spree, including:

  • Paramount Group (NYSE:PGRE), an office REIT for ~$1.6B,
  • $1B in home improvement loans from Upgrade, Inc, a fintech company that offers affordable and responsible credit and mobile banking to mainstream consumers, and
  • Crestline Management, an alternative investment manager with approximately $17 billion in assets under management.

This development lends credence to RITM's ongoing diversification efforts away from the existing mortgage origination/servicing business as they attempt to grow their portfolio in the alternative asset management rather aggressively, albeit by diluting shareholders and raising credit through third party capital/partners.

Upon the closing of these acquisitions, the mREIT expects to report $109B in pro forma investable assets, up moderately from the last reported sum of $80B in FQ2'25 earnings call, with the management expecting to draw on financing facilities of $1.14B to arrive at a proforma cash position of $1.27B.

It is unsurprising that RITM has embarked on this path, since the market is entering a lower cost borrowing environment in 2026, with it presenting headwinds to its intermediate term prospects.

While the value of mortgage servicing rights has previously risen as interest rates have risen over the past few years, it is apparent that things may no longer be the same, with MSRs likely to decline in value as the Feds continue their rate cuts.

This devaluation trend may also be why RITM has doubled down on their diversification efforts while attempting to drive higher valuation through their "differentiated model and operating platforms."

RITM's Discounted Valuations Unwarranted

For reference, RITM remains inherently discounted as an mREIT at TTM Price/Book valuations of 0.93x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 5.48x, compared to the sector median at 1.34x and 11.52x, respectively.

The same conclusion may also be derived when comparing to its mREIT peers, including:

  • Annaly Capital Management (NLY) at 1.24x/8.20x,
  • AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) at 1.33x/7.70x,
  • Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) at 1.14x/15.37x,
  • Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) at 1.19x/10.20x, and
  • PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (PMT) at 0.86x/11.19x, respectively,

with it apparent that RITM offers a value-oriented mREIT investment thesis - as similarly observed in its notably lower, single digits forward dividend yields of 8.39% (compared to its peers' double digits yields).

While some may argue that RITM's discounted valuations may be attributed to its frozen dividend payouts at an annualized sum of $1 since late 2021 and the notably low EAD payout ratio at 46.2% in FQ3'25 (inline YoY), I beg to differ, since a similar trend has been observed in some of its mREIT peers in varying degrees, including:

  • NLY with a recent payout hike by +7.7%, EAD coverage ratio of 95.8%, and forward dividend yields of 11.74%,
  • AGNC with stagnant payouts since early 2020, 46.1%, and 12.21%,
  • DX with 3Y dividend growth rate at +9%, 42.5%, and 14.13%,
  • TWO with 3Y dividend decline at -16.81%, -44.1%, and 10.14%, and
  • PMT with 3Y dividend decline at -4.03%, 72.7%, and 12.08%, respectively,

with it apparent that RITM's discounted valuations may be unwarranted, especially since its ongoing diversification may trigger the alternative assets comprising a larger share of its overall pro forma investable assets at an estimated sum of ~56.8% in FQ4'25 (+11.8 points QoQ).

Metrics To Monitor Entering FQ4'25 Earnings Call

As a result of their promising diversification progress, I urge readers to closely monitor RITM's upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call on February 3, 2026, since it remains to be seen if the asset management revenues may eventually comprise a larger sum of its overall revenues, currently at a measly sum of 7.6% in FQ3'25 (-0.5 points YoY).

On the one hand, I urge readers to temper their expectations, since I expect to see moderate volatility in its MSR hedging results and net interest income (based on interest income minus interest expense/warehouse line fees) attributed to the three Fed rate cut in late 2025, with it likely to present near-term noise in its top/bottom-line performance.

At the same time, given the intensified diversification process through numerous acquisitions and the potentially higher integration costs in the near-term, I am of the opinion that RITM's dividend payouts are likely to remain flat ahead, with it likely to compress its valuations for a little longer.

This is especially since the management is funding its recent acquisitions through a mix of balance sheet and third party capital, with shareholder return likely taking a back seat as they aim to "deploy capital at, call it, 15% to 20% returns," as observed in the excellent return on equity of 18% in FQ3'25 (in line QoQ/YoY).

On the other hand, I am of the opinion that the lower borrowing cost environment may trigger RITM's expanded new mortgage origination/mortgage refinancing numbers on a QoQ/YoY basis, as similarly reported by Mortgage Bankers Association.

As a result of numerous potential outcomes entering FQ4'25 earnings call, I urge readers to tune in on February 3, 2026 indeed.

RITM Remains A Great Income Buy, Pending Upgrading

For now, RITM has mostly traded sideways at the $11s since 2024, offering interested investors great insight into its future stock price movement, pending an upward re-rating in its valuations.

I am of the opinion that at a time of Fed rate cut, the mREIT's forward dividend yields of 8.39% already offer interested and patient investors a rich quarterly income investment thesis.

Upon the speculative upward re-rating in its FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations nearer to the sector median of 11.52x, readers may look forward to an outsized capital appreciation prospect to my bull-case long-term price target of $27.10 as well, based on the consensus FY2027 EAD estimates of $2.36.

As a result of its well-balanced and secure dividend payouts in the near term along with the potentially rich, more than doubling upside potential over the next few years, thanks to the aggressive diversification efforts, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the RITM stock here.

data.title: Hints Of U.S. Treasury Intervention To Support The Yen
data.description:
The Man Who Helped Break The Bank Of England May Save The Bank Of Japan

Before becoming U.S. Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent built his reputation in the hedge fund world. Early in his career, he rose to prominence at Soros Management, where he contributed to the firm’s legendary bet against the British Pound

Using Bessent’s research that the pound was overvalued, Soros Management made a $10 billion wager that the Bank of England would not continue to support their currency. It all came to a head on Wednesday, September 16, 1992, when the Bank of England abandoned using reserves to buy pounds, causing the currency to crash. Bessent’s firm made a tidy $1 billion profit.

This period is famously referred to as “Black Wednesday,” when Soros Management “Broke the Bank of England.”

I highlight Bessent’s risk taking nature in my recent Seeking Alpha article “Secretary Bessent Is Running The U.S. Treasury Like A Hedge Fund.”

Well, Bessent is back in the currency markets again. Only this time, as U.S Treasury Secretary, he is aiding the Bank of Japan ("BOJ") in supporting their currency.

The BOJ has a huge problem. They have been very slow in normalizing their monetary policy after an extended period of extreme easing. They are dealing with a delicate balance between new fiscal policy initiatives to prop up the economy while inflation remains persistently high. This has resulted in rising bond yields and a weak yen.

Possible Yen Intervention

There has been a flurry of activity in Japanese markets this past week. Prime Minister, Sanae Takaichi, who has only been in office for three months, proposed a new fiscal measure to temporarily eliminate the 8% consumption tax on food for two years. While this measure is appealing to voters who are dealing with the high cost of groceries, it creates a huge hole in the budget, with no clear solution as to how the tax cut would be funded.

Market concern that the revenue loss would be filled by new government borrowing caused long bond yields to spike, as investors worried that new debt would only increase Japan’s bloated borrowing. At a total debt to GDP level of over 250%, Japan is already the most indebted country in the world.

The market uncertainty was exacerbated by Takaichi calling for a snap election to be held on February 8th, only 18 days away. By dissolving the Lower House, whose term was to run until October 2028, the Prime Minister is hoping to capitalize on her popularity by strengthening her party and its coalition’s position in the parliament.

The foreign exchange value of the yen weakened leading up to these events.

On Friday, the BOJ announced that in an 8-1 vote, they decided to hold the policy rate at the current 0.75%. The lone dissenter voted to raise the policy rate an additional 25 basis points to 1.00% due to his concern about the elevated level of inflation.

At the post-meeting press briefing, BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda was noncommittal about when the next rate hike might occur. With regard to bonds though, Ueda stated “Long-term interest rates are rising at quite a fast pace” and that “we are ready to take nimble action to cope with exceptional, unusual moves.”

As seen in the intra-day chart below, initially, the yen weakened to 159.25, on concern the BOJ wasn’t tightening enough to combat inflation and the new fiscal stimulus.

This was the yen’s highest level since June 2024 when the BOJ last intervened to support the currency at 160.00. It then rallied unexpectedly in anticipation that another intervention might occur.

The rally was strengthened several hours later when in New York, buzz began spreading that the New York Fed, at the behest of the Scott Bessent’s Treasury, was doing a “Rate Check” on what levels they could purchase Japanese Yen.

A rate check is considered a high-level warning that the government is concerned about the exchange rate and that a currency intervention might occur.

Sometimes, though, a rate check can be used as a way to give notice of an undesirable move in the currency, and cause positioning to create a reversal, without actually intervening.

Although the Fed is authorized to intervene in the foreign exchange (FX) markets, it is a rare event. There have only been three FX interventions in the past 30 years. The most recent was in 2011. Usually, the intervention is conducted in coordination with the central bank of the target currency.

At the end of the day, between the speculation in Japan about currency intervention, and the buzz in New York about the Treasury’s rate check, the yen rallied 1.75% to 155.63, This was the biggest 1-day surge since August.

Bigger Picture

Japan, like the rest of the G-7 economies, uses a target inflation rate of 2.0%.

CPI ex fresh foods and energy, otherwise known as Core Core CPI, is the BOJ’s preferred measure of inflation. As seen in the chart below, it broke above the desired 2.0% rate in October 2022. It has been above target ever since. Core Core for December 2025 came in at 2.9%.

While the Federal Reserve began Quantitative Tightening in March 2022l, the BOJ has been behind the curve. It took the BOJ an additional two years to follow suit. BOJ was the last man standing with their Negative Interest Rate Policy, which finally ended in March 2024.

Since then, the two central banks have been moving in opposite directions. The BOJ has raised their policy rate 4 times by a total of 85 basis points to the current level of 0.75%. Meanwhile, over the past 16 months the Fed has cut rates 6 times by a total of 175 basis points, to the current level of 3.75%. The yield spread differential between the BOJ’s and the Fed’s policy rates, at 300 basis points, is the narrowest it has been since August 2022.

On the longer end, Japanese Government Bond (JGB) yields have risen more significantly. The following chart shows the JGB yield curve when the BOJ ended their Negative Interest Rate Policy and today. While 3-month securities have risen by 79 basis points, 10-year JGB yields have risen 153 basis points and 30-year JGB yields are up 181 basis points.

The 10 yr JGB hit 2.38% this week, a 27-year high, and the 30 yr JGB set a new record at 3.92%. Additionally, 40-year JGB yields rose above 4.00% for the first time since they were introduced in 2007. Long yields surged as much as 35 basis points on Tuesday when the market was spooked by PM Takaichi’s stimulus package of spending increases and the consumption tax cuts, as well as her call for the snap election on February 8th.

Treasury Secretary Bessent Ties Rise In Treasury Yields To JGB Selloff

Speaking from Davos, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that he believed the recent weakness in Treasury bonds is tied to the selloff in JGBs.

He downplayed that the threats from Denmark of selling their Treasury securities in protest of the US position on Greenland were of any significance. While Bessent was curt with his views on Denmark, he was not wrong. Denmark only holds $10 billion in Treasury securities, a mere 0.1% of the $9 trillion of Treasury securities held by foreigners.

What went unsaid, however, was the risk of Japan selling their Treasury holdings in order to support their currency. Compared with Denmark, Japan is a very significant owner of Treasury bonds. Their holdings of $1.2 trillion in Treasuries make then the largest foreign owner of Treasury securities.

Perhaps Bessent is helping out the BOJ through Treasury’s rate check, so they don’t have to sell their Treasuries.

We know Bessent is worried about the JGB influence on Treasuries, because a key component of his strategy at Treasury for overweighting Treasury bill issuance, is to have longer rates come down, in parallel, with short rates. That way Treasury can term out their debt issuance into longer maturities at lower rates.

So far that hasn’t happened.

Since the Fed began lowering the Fed Funds Rate in September 2024, there have been six rate cuts totaling 175 basis points in rate reduction, to 3.75%. During that same period, the yield on the 10-year Treasury note (US10Y) has risen 63 basis points to 4.26%.

While at the beginning of the period we had an inverted 10-year Treasury to Fed Funds spread, by -187 basis points, it has now flipped to be positively sloped by 51 basis points.

Deteriorating Liquidity In The JGB Market

One of the problems in the JGB market is that no one wants to own them.

The largest holder of JGBs is the BOJ, and they are paring their positions through their version of Quantitative Tightening. In 2010, the BOJ owned less than 10% of the JGB market, but over the ensuing 13-year period, when they were executing their aggressive Quantitative Easing policy, their JGB holdings grew dramatically. A key element of their aggressive easing was their Yield Curve Control Policy, where they continuously purchased JGB’s to defend the band around their 0.0% target yield for 10-year JGBs. These continuous purchases swelled the BOJ’s JGB position to 54% of total JGBs outstanding.

Since their QT program began, though, the BOJ’s JGB holdings have declined to a 50% share of the total outstanding. No one has stepped up to fill the void, hence the sharp rise in yields.

The deterioration in liquidity is best exemplified by the Bloomberg JGB Liquidity Index. The index measures the average yield difference to fair value. When the difference is small, the market is considered quite liquid. As the average yield widens from fair value, the index signals distressed conditions and a deterioration in liquidity.

During normal times, the index hovers around 1 basis point. Since the BOJ embarked on their QT program, and stopped buying JGBs, the index has deteriorated significantly. The bond market selloff described earlier, caused the index to spike to a record high of 10 basis points, before settling back to a still illiquid level of 8.7 basis points.

This is a tremendous source of concern now, as the Prime Minister’s new policies will cause debt issuance to increase into this illiquid market.

Conclusion

Treasury Secretary Bessent, who began his career making large currency bets by helping Soros Management “Break the Bank of England,” is returning to his hedge fund ways.

On Friday, at the behest of Secretary Bessent’s U.S. Treasury, the New York Fed conducted a rate check among currency dealers to determine where they might be able to buy yen. The rate check is a loud signal that they are not happy with the current exchange rates, and an indication that currency intervention might occur.

The rate check caused the yen to rally 1.75%, it’s largest one day move in five months.

Last week, while attending the World Economic Forum in Davos, Secretary Bessent indicated that he thought the weakness in the U.S. Treasury market had been impacted by the selloff in JGBs. He also stated that “I’ve been in touch with my economic counterpart in Japan, and I am sure that they will begin saying the things that will calm the market down.”

While President Trump has been railing about the Fed cutting the Fed Funds rate, Secretary Bessent has been more focused on the 10-year Treasury yield coming down, as that has more impact on mortgage rates, car loans and business lending.

The Treasury has made a big bet on this occurring, as it has been funding the government’s borrowing needs with Treasury Bills, because Bessent feels long rates are too high to term out the government’s borrowing, thereby locking in a high borrowing cost.

This hasn’t happened yet, though, as while the Fed Funds Rate has declined by 175 basis points since the Fed began easing, the 10-year Treasury yield has actually risen by 63 basis points.

By conducting a rate check on the yen, the Treasury is helping the BOJ support their currency during a period of internal turmoil in Japan. But it is also moving to protect the Treasury market from possible sales of Treasury securities by Japan, the largest foreign holder of U.S. government debt. Another impact, of course, of weakening the dollar in support of the yen, is to improve our trade exports.

data.title: Cipher Mining: Execution Replaces Speculation In 2026
data.description:
Cipher Mining (CIFR) has been one of the Bitcoin miners executing well these past years. I've been long CIFR for the past few years. My past coverage of the company reflects this stance, which all carried Buy ratings. In between the coverages, there have been good runs and sometimes retracements. In my last coverage I highlighted the point that Cipher Mining’s rerating story isn't over yet.

CIFR is up a lot from 2024, but currently off 2025 highs. In this piece, I'd look at what lies ahead for 2026 as a Bitcoin miner and a HPC/AI play, and also touch on the HPC deals secured last year and the implication of those deals into the present thesis.

Cipher Mining - From Pipeline to Paychecks in 2026

Cipher Mining is one of the HPC/AI plays expecting an operational inflection point in 2026. The market at the moment has remained muted since the HPC/AI hype late last year for the miners that secured deals.

​They all rerated in Q4, but have now since mostly retraced those gains. CIFR itself surged to a 52-week high of ~$25 on the news of the $3 billion 10-year Fluidstack lease deal last September, then followed by the $5.5 billion AWS deal, before retracing following the issuance of senior secured notes totalling $1.73 billion in mid November.

I'd like to say that the market seems exhausted in assigning premium to miners turned HPC plays based on just announced deals, pipeline capacity, and projected cash flow discounted into today's share price. I highlighted these in an article published here on Seeking Alpha last Friday covering Hut 8 Corp. (HUT), another miner turned HPC play. While each of these miners have secured what looks like similar multi-billion HPC deals, nuances in the deals themselves, as well as each miner's equity composition (of which I'm currently weighing their specific exposure to Bitcoin volatility through their balance sheets), and projected net operating income [NOI] for the duration of the deal are the specific metrics I'm looking at as I analyze these companies.

​In my latest HUT article, I downgraded HUT from a Buy to a Hold, despite HUT having seen similar capacity development and deals win as the rest of the HPC pivot companies. The main reason was the longer-dated timeline for their River Bend site energization compared to peers, which was compounded by the company's exposure to Bitcoin as it holds over 13,000 BTC on the balance sheet. These present a double headwind for the company, making 2026 a potentially muted year for HUT.

​In contrast, as I analyze Cipher Mining going into 2026, I'd be maintaining a Buy for CIFR for the singular reason that the checks for the HPC lease from AWS are projected to hit the books from August this year, and 168 MW Fluidstack hosting capacity at Barber Lake expects to commence rent in September. Impressive top line numbers making the headline are a first place to watch for the next catalysts.

​I’ve talked about the sentiment of the current market, which has apparently moved beyond maintaining momentum based on just pipeline capacity or capacity under exclusivity. Those were last year's catalysts. 2026 is the execution year, when HPC pivots show actual top-line impact on financials.

To project what's coming for Cipher, I'd make some assumptions. Take the Cipher's base mining revenue to be ~$55 million per quarter (I derived that using the average revenue of three quarters already reported in FY25); annualized, this gives a steady mining run rate of ~$220 million. I'm quite positive the company can now produce that amount of quarterly revenue from mining as a steady run rate till the next halving, with current hashrate at 23.5 EH/s and mining efficiency looking good at 16.8 J/Th.

Now layer in the HPC contracts. Cipher has roughly $8.5 billion of contracted revenue from the AWS and Fluidstack deals. If this revenue is recognized on a straight-line basis over a 13-year weighted contract life, it implies ~$650 million in annual HPC revenue (calculated as $8.5 billion for the combined deal divided by 13 years). And would put total sales from both mining and HPC to about $870 million. Note that my estimate is still a conservative one as I use a straight line assumption for the modeling.

Cipher's adjusted EBITDA margin improved greatly in 2025. It was just around 12% in Q1 2025 on $49 million revenue and $6.1 million adjusted earnings, but jumped to ~60% in Q3 on a $72.0 million revenue and ~$41 million adjusted EBITDA in Q3. I believe higher adjusted EBITDA is sustainable because it was driven by the upgrade of the mining fleets to from older Bitmain T21s to S21 xp Antminers. Economies of scale are likely to stay intact in the meantime with factors like network difficulty and Bitcoin price not moving too much.

For the purpose of my modeling for projected earnings, I'd however factor in volatility and go with a conservative ~50% adjusted EBITDA for the mining operations moving forward, which I believe the company can sustain considering the improved 16.8 J/Th average efficiency the S21 xp Antminers has brought to the mining fleet.

Valuation is bound to shift from Cipher's current life as a Bitcoin miner and when the HPC checks hit in H2. In the deals, management is guiding for 80 - 85% NOI margin, which I believe is very well within range, as the AWS deal is a triple N, meaning the clients take care of the biggest three costs (power, cooling, and facility costs). For this modelling, I'll be treating NOI margin as EBITDA margin which is safe enough considering low overhead and other operational levers in the HPC lease deal, which when accounted for won't skew our EBITDA from NOI estimate materially.

At $6.94 billion market cap at present and $870M projected 2027 sales, CIFR trades at ~7.9x sales, meaning at current market cap CIFR is trading around 7.9x projected sales. A ~7.9x forward sales multiple for a company with 75%+ blended EBITDA margins and contracted revenue from AWS and Google can be considered modest. Traditional data center providers like Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Reality (DLR) typically trade in the low mid teens to sales, but they have much slower growth rates as they are well established firms beyond the rapid growth phase. The case for CIFR is that it is combining high-margin HPC revenue with optionality in Bitcoin upside and potential for rapid growth, which are visible through the pipeline capacity and the AWS and Fluidstack deals themselves which have clauses that allow expansion down the line.

Using the current $6.78 billion EV and our blended EBITDA projection of $662.5 million from both segments, implies an EV/EBITDA [fwd] of 10.2x (gotten from EV divided by projected EBITDA), and the valuation gets even more attractive.

At 10.2x 2027 EBITDA, CIFR is being priced as if the HPC deals are still high-risk speculations. The market is ignoring the fact that the AWS and Fluidstack contracts are legally binding and the sites already in build-out, the market is underpricing the near certainty of the coming cash flows.

As the HPC revenues start to come in, if CIFR catches up to the Sales multiple of data center provider peers (moving from 8x to 10 - 12x), that would imply a ~25% upside for the rerating from current stock price. If CIFR catches up to the EBITDA multiple (moving from 10.2x to 20 - 30x), the stock would more than double.

Risks

The risk I worry about most at this stage is construction and energization slippage risk. As I have noted with Hut 8 and why I downgraded to a Hold because of longer-dated energization timelines, while peers are already locked in for HPC revenue just months away. The market punishes longer-dated timelines. Any hint that the August 2026 for AWS site launch and October for Fluidstack site launch is sliding into 2027 might trigger a sharp CIFR sell-off.

Takeaway

The market moved with momentum on the AWS deal news in November and is now under-appreciating it in January. The gap between current prices around $17 and the top last November around $25 is the execution premium that will likely return as we get closer to that first lease payment in 2H this year.

We can do as much rough math as we want, but unless the HPC lease payments don’t start hitting the top line in September/October, CIFR will likely trade as a momentum stock in the latter part of this year, to be driven by the headline top-line numbers from the HPC deals.
